metadata.title: Scope the MVP: ONE n8n Workflow (15 of 30 pts)
metadata.topic: INFO 7375 ASSIGNMENT 2 — MVP SCOPE: ONE N8N WORKFLOW (15 PTS)
metadata.playlist: Plan It Like a PM — Your Madison PRD
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 2 brief — Plan Your Madison Project Like a Pro

beats.narration_text: E6. Design is infinite; Week 3 is not. This is where you pick the one thing you will actually build — and honestly name what you will not. The MVP section is worth 15 points. If you scope too wide here, you will not finish in Week 3, and the grade goes with it. The most common way students lose points on this section is not designing something too simple — it is designing something unrealistically large and then not being able to demo it.

shot.topic: Episode 6 — Scope the MVP: ONE n8n Workflow (15 pts)
output: One n8n workflow — not all three agents
Explicit out-of-scope list — scope discipline is graded

beats.narration_text: Before you design the workflow, get the terminology straight. Madison and n8n are two different things. Madison is the design framework — it describes the agent architecture you planned in Episode 5: the five layers, the named components, the orchestration layer. n8n is the build tool — it is a separate visual workflow-automation platform where one of your agent's logic becomes a runnable workflow of connected nodes. They are paired in this assignment, but they are different products. In n8n, a node is one step. It could be a trigger — the thing that starts the workflow — or an action like making an HTTP request, or a transformation that filters or reshapes data. You wire nodes together: input node receives something, hands it to a process node, which hands its output to the next node. The MVP question is: which one agent from Episode 5 becomes a real, running n8n workflow in Week 3? Not all three — one.

shot.artifactTitle: n8n vs Madison — two different things
shot.artifactHeading: n8n defined — separate from Madison, same assignment
artifactLines: Madison = the design framework: five layers, named components, orchestration
n8n    = the build tool: one agent's logic becomes a runnable node workflow
n8n node = one step: trigger · action · transformation
Wire them: input node → process node(s) → output node
MVP question: which ONE agent from Episode 5 runs as a real n8n workflow in Week 3?
shot.sparkLine: Madison designs it — n8n builds it — one agent — one workflow

beats.narration_text: Picking the right agent to build is not a creative decision, it is a feasibility decision. Three criteria tell you which agent wins. First: the input is a real, available data source — not 'we will get data later,' but something you can actually point to today, whether that is a public API, a Google Sheet, a form, a webhook, or a file. Second: the processing can be done with n8n's native nodes plus an HTTP call or an AI node — you are not rebuilding a machine learning pipeline from scratch inside n8n. Third: the output is something observable — a file, a webhook response, a database row, a sent email, a Slack message. If you cannot demo the output, you cannot prove the workflow ran. Here is the scope-selection prompt.

shot.topic: Pick the one agent to build in Week 3
shot.command: My three Madison agents from the architecture section: [AGENT 1 — job], [AGENT 2 — job], [AGENT 3 — job]. For a Week 3 MVP in n8n with 3–5 nodes, which single agent's job is the most concrete and achievable? Criteria: (1) the input is a real, available data source — not 'we will get data later', (2) the process can be done with n8n's native nodes plus an HTTP call or an AI node, (3) the output is something observable — a file, a webhook response, a database row, a sent email. Recommend ONE agent, state why it wins on those three criteria, and flag what makes the other two out of scope for Week 3.
shot.runningText: scoping the MVP — one agent, not all three…
output: Why it wins: input available · process achievable · output observable
Other two agents: why each is out of scope for Week 3

beats.narration_text: Once you have picked the one agent, design the node sequence. Three to five nodes is the MVP ceiling — every node you add is another thing that can break during the Week 3 demo. For each node, you need three things: the real n8n node type name, what that node does in this specific workflow, and what data it receives versus what it sends to the next node. The input-process-output description should be two sentences: one sentence for the data coming in, one sentence for what leaves. One important note: look up the node names at docs.n8n.io before you put them on the board. The AI sometimes invents node names that sound plausible but do not exist in n8n. If you put a node name on your diagram that does not exist, the instructor will check and you will lose credibility on the whole section. Verify first, then commit.

shot.topic: Design the 3–5 node workflow for my MVP agent
shot.command: My MVP agent: [AGENT NAME — job in one sentence]. Design a 3–5 node n8n workflow for this agent. For each node: (1) the n8n node type — use real n8n node names such as HTTP Request, Set, AI Agent, Google Sheets, Webhook, Code, Filter, Schedule Trigger, (2) what it does in this specific workflow, (3) what data it receives as input and what it sends as output. Then describe the input → process → output flow in two sentences.
output: Node 1: real n8n node type · what it does · input received · output sent
Node 2: real n8n node type · what it does · input received · output sent

beats.narration_text: The out-of-scope list is not optional. The rubric explicitly grades scope discipline, and the way you demonstrate scope discipline is by writing down what you are not building. Three categories belong in the list. First, the two agents you are not building in Week 3 — name them by name. Second, any data source that would be needed for the full system but is not available yet — if getting access to that data would take longer than one week, it is out of scope. Third, any feature from your user stories that the MVP version will not cover — if a story from Episode 4 requires the full three-agent architecture, it does not belong in the Week 3 demo. Here is a working template: Out of scope for Week 3 MVP — not building: Agent X, Agent Y. Data not available: Z. Features deferred: W. Write those three lines. Do not skip them.

artifactLines: The out-of-scope list is how you show the instructor you understand what is achievable in one week.
Required items:
· Name the two agents you are NOT building in Week 3
· Name any data source needed but not yet available
· Name any user-story feature the MVP version will not cover
Template:
Out of scope for Week 3 MVP:
Not building: [Agent X], [Agent Y]
Data not available: [Z]
Features deferred: [W]
shot.sparkLine: name what you will not build → show you understand the constraint

beats.narration_text: Three excellence moves that separate a good MVP section from a great one. First: real node research. Open docs.n8n.io, find the specific node you plan to use, confirm it does what you need it to do, and put the actual node name from the documentation on your diagram — not a guess, not a paraphrase, the real name. Second: a data schema between two nodes. Show what Node 2 actually receives from Node 1 — the JSON fields and their types. This is the same annotation move from Episode 5, applied to the n8n workflow. Third: name the specific trigger. What starts this workflow? A Schedule Trigger set to run every Monday morning? A Webhook that fires when a form is submitted? A manual run for the demo? The trigger determines whether the Week 3 demo can actually be run live, on command. Name it.

shot.artifactHeading: Excellence — real node names, data schema, trigger
artifactLines: Excellence move 1: open docs.n8n.io · find the node · verify it does what you need · use the exact name
Excellence move 2: show the data schema between two nodes — JSON fields + types (same annotation as E5)
Excellence move 3: name the specific trigger — Schedule Trigger · Webhook · manual
The trigger determines whether you can demo the workflow live in Week 3 — name it early
shot.sparkLine: verified node names → data schema → named trigger → demo-ready

beats.narration_text: Four traps. Trap one: a workflow with 15 nodes. 'I will build everything' is the most common reason for a Week 3 demo that does not run. Three to five nodes is the MVP ceiling — every node you add is another failure point during the demo. Trap two: 'I will build all three agents.' That is exactly what the out-of-scope list is for. One agent, one workflow. The other two agents are Week 4 or Week 5. Trap three: no explicit out-of-scope list. 'Out of scope' is a named section in your deliverable, not an implied assumption. Write it, label it, put three items in it. Trap four: n8n node names you have not looked up. If your diagram says 'AI Marketing Optimizer node,' that node does not exist in n8n. The instructor will check docs.n8n.io. Verify before you commit.

artifactLines: Trap 1 — 15-node workflow: 3–5 nodes is the ceiling; every extra node is a demo failure point
Trap 2 — Build all three agents: one agent, one workflow — the other two are future weeks
Trap 3 — No out-of-scope list: it is a named section, not an implied assumption — write it
Trap 4 — Unverified node names: if the node is not on docs.n8n.io, it does not exist in n8n
Fix: 3–5 real nodes · one agent · explicit out-of-scope list · verify every node name
shot.sparkLine: 3–5 nodes → one agent → out-of-scope listed → all node names verified

beats.narration_text: The rules for the MVP section. One agent, one workflow — not all three. Three to five real n8n node names, verified at docs.n8n.io. The input-process-output flow described in two sentences. An explicit out-of-scope list with at least three items: the two agents not being built, any unavailable data source, and any deferred user-story feature. Excellence means naming the specific trigger and putting a data schema on at least one node-to-node arrow. Scope discipline is the whole grade for this section. Pick one thing, describe it precisely, and name what you are not building.

artifactLines: One agent · one workflow (not all three)
3–5 real n8n node names — verified at docs.n8n.io
Input → process → output described in two sentences
Explicit out-of-scope list: 3+ items (agents not built · unavailable data · deferred features)
Excellence: trigger named + data schema on at least one node-to-node arrow
Scope discipline is the whole grade — pick one thing, describe it precisely

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take the three agents you designed in Episode 5 and the scope-selection prompt — pick the one agent whose job is actually buildable in a week. Then use this prompt to design the node sequence. Before you commit anything to the Figma board, open docs.n8n.io and verify every node name you plan to use. That check takes five minutes and protects you from the most common credibility loss in this section. Write the out-of-scope list before you finalize the node diagram — knowing what you are not building clarifies what you are.

shot.topic: Scope your MVP — one agent, one n8n workflow
shot.command: My three Madison agents: [AGENT 1 — job], [AGENT 2 — job], [AGENT 3 — job]. My MVP agent for Week 3: [AGENT NAME — job]. Input data source (real and available): [INPUT SOURCE]. Desired output (observable): [OUTPUT]. Design a 3–5 node n8n workflow for this agent. For each node: (1) the real n8n node type name, (2) what it does in this workflow, (3) input received and output sent. Name the specific trigger node. Describe the input → process → output flow in two sentences. Then write the out-of-scope list: the two agents not being built, any data source not yet available, and any user-story feature deferred beyond Week 3.
shot.runningText: scope one agent — then verify every node name at docs.n8n.io…
output: Trigger node: type · what starts the workflow
3–5 nodes: real n8n node type · job · input → output
Out-of-scope list: agents not built · unavailable data · deferred features

beats.narration_text: Scope the MVP: ONE n8n Workflow.
